commit 2ff19b031c2f62bb18bc5818d6d8b6553e523364
Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

mm/huge_memory: fix folio isn't locked in softleaf_to_folio()

This commit adds an smp_rmb() memory barrier in softleaf_to_folio() and
softleaf_to_page() to fix a race condition between mTHP splitting and
zap_nonpresent_ptes() where page flags of tail pages could be read before
the tail page becomes non-compound.

> diff --git a/include/linux/leafops.h b/include/linux/leafops.h
> index a9ff94b744f2..dd4130b7cb7f 100644
> --- a/include/linux/leafops.h
> +++ b/include/linux/leafops.h

[ ... ]

> @@ -363,6 +363,19 @@ static inline unsigned long softleaf_to_pfn(softleaf_t entry)
>  	return swp_offset(entry) & SWP_PFN_MASK;
>  }
>  
> +static inline void softleaf_migration_entry_check(softleaf_t entry,
> +			struct folio *folio)
> +{
> +	/* See __split_folio_to_order() comment */
> +	smp_rmb();
> +
> +	/*
> +	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
> +	 * corresponding page is locked
> +	 */
> +	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio));
> +}
> +
>  /**
>   * softleaf_to_page() - Obtains struct page for PFN encoded within leaf entry.
>   * @entry: Leaf entry, softleaf_has_pfn(@entry) must return true.
> @@ -374,11 +387,8 @@ static inline struct page *softleaf_to_page(softleaf_t entry)
>  	struct page *page = pfn_to_page(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
>  
>  	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
> -	/*
> -	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
> -	 * corresponding page is locked
> -	 */
> -	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(softleaf_is_migration(entry) && !PageLocked(page));
> +	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
> +		softleaf_migration_entry_check(entry, page_folio(page));
>  
>  	return page;
>  }

Does this smp_rmb() inside softleaf_to_page() (via the call to
softleaf_migration_entry_check) fully protect callers that subsequently
re-derive the folio from the returned page?

If a caller uses softleaf_to_page() and then immediately calls
page_folio(page) or PageAnon(page), that performs a new read of
page->compound_head after the smp_rmb() has already executed.

Could the CPU speculatively reorder subsequent reads (like folio->mapping
or folio->flags) before this second read of compound_head, recreating the
exact read-read reordering race this commit intended to fix?

For example, if __split_huge_pmd_locked() or smaps_pte_entry() uses
softleaf_to_page() and then checks PageAnon(page), it might read a stale
TAIL_MAPPING before the new compound_head.

Should these callers be updated to use softleaf_to_folio() instead of
softleaf_to_page() so they use the safely-synchronized folio pointer?

commit 2ff19b031c2f62bb18bc5818d6d8b6553e523364
Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>
Date:   Thu Mar 19 01:25:41 2026 +0000

    mm/huge_memory: fix folio isn't locked in softleaf_to_folio()
    
    On arm64 server, we found folio that get from migration entry isn't locked
    in softleaf_to_folio(). This issue triggers when mTHP splitting and
    zap_nonpresent_ptes() races, and the root cause is lack of memory barrier
    in softleaf_to_folio(). The race is as follows:
    
            CPU0                                             CPU1
    
    deferred_split_scan()                              zap_nonpresent_ptes()
      lock folio
      split_folio()
        unmap_folio()
          change ptes to migration entries
        __split_folio_to_order()                         softleaf_to_folio()
          set flags(including PG_locked) for tail pages    folio = pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry))
          smp_wmb()                                        VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio))
          prep_compound_page() for tail pages
    
    In __split_folio_to_order(), smp_wmb() guarantees page flags of tail pages
    are visible before the tail page becomes non-compound. smp_wmb() should
    be paired with smp_rmb() in softleaf_to_folio(), which is missed. As a
    result, if zap_nonpresent_ptes() accesses migration entry that stores
    tail pfn, softleaf_to_folio() may see the updated compound_head of tail
    page before page->flags.
    
    To fix it, add missing smp_rmb() if the softleaf entry is migration entry
    in softleaf_to_folio() and softleaf_to_page().
    
    Fixes: e9b61f19858a ("thp: reintroduce split_huge_page()")
    Signed-off-by: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

diff --git a/include/linux/leafops.h b/include/linux/leafops.h
index a9ff94b744f2..dd4130b7cb7f 100644
--- a/include/linux/leafops.h
+++ b/include/linux/leafops.h
@@ -363,6 +363,19 @@ static inline unsigned long softleaf_to_pfn(softleaf_t entry)
 	return swp_offset(entry) & SWP_PFN_MASK;
 }
 
+static inline void softleaf_migration_entry_check(softleaf_t entry,
+			struct folio *folio)
+{
+	/* See __split_folio_to_order() comment */
+	smp_rmb();
+
+	/*
+	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
+	 * corresponding page is locked
+	 */
+	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio));
+}
+
 /**
  * softleaf_to_page() - Obtains struct page for PFN encoded within leaf entry.
  * @entry: Leaf entry, softleaf_has_pfn(@entry) must return true.
@@ -374,11 +387,8 @@ static inline struct page *softleaf_to_page(softleaf_t entry)
 	struct page *page = pfn_to_page(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
 
 	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
-	/*
-	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
-	 * corresponding page is locked
-	 */
-	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(softleaf_is_migration(entry) && !PageLocked(page));
+	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
+		softleaf_migration_entry_check(entry, page_folio(page));
 
 	return page;
 }
@@ -394,12 +404,8 @@ static inline struct folio *softleaf_to_folio(softleaf_t entry)
 	struct folio *folio = pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
 
 	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
-	/*
-	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
-	 * corresponding folio is locked.
-	 */
-	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(softleaf_is_migration(entry) &&
-			!folio_test_locked(folio));
+	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
+		softleaf_migration_entry_check(entry, folio);
 
 	return folio;
 }

    "thought": "**Analysis of the Memory Barrier Introduction in Migration Entry Handling**

Okay, so this commit's introducing `smp_rmb()` within `softleaf_to_page()` and `softleaf_to_folio()` when dealing with migration entries. It looks like we had a race on arm64 servers. The issue was that when splitting mTHP and `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` raced, the folio from a migration entry wasn't always locked in `softleaf_to_folio()`.

Let me break this down. In `__split_folio_to_order()`, after the page is split, we set flags, including `PG_locked`, on the tail pages of a compound page. Importantly, `smp_wmb()` is used there after these flags are set. The author is ensuring that the flag updates are visible *before* the tail page is treated as a non-compound page. The original problem was the lack of a corresponding `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_folio()`.

Initially, I was worried about whether there was an issue with pfn_folio() and the compound_head being updated. But the commit is adding `smp_rmb()` after `pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry))`, which is key. I'm using `pfn_folio()` which eventually reads the `compound_head`. The author put the `smp_rmb()` *after* `pfn_folio()` but before `folio_test_locked()`, and this *is* correct. `folio_test_locked()` is the function that reads the `folio->flags` that we care about.

The `smp_rmb()` enforces the proper ordering! The first part of the code reads the `compound_head`, and then the barrier occurs, and only then do we read `folio->flags`. So, it seems like we're good. If `compound_head` has been updated and points to the page itself, the barrier ensures that we see the updated `PG_locked` bit set by the other CPU before `compound_head` was cleared, which is what `folio_test_locked` expects.

Overall, it's a well-reasoned fix. No concerns. It directly addresses the race condition by inserting the necessary memory barrier.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of the Memory Ordering Issue in Folio Splitting**

Okay, so this commit's about ensuring proper memory ordering when dealing with folios that are in the process of being split. The core problem is that a read of the `compound_head` field (which determines if a page is part of a larger folio) might happen concurrently with a read of the `page->flags` field, especially when accessing the page through a migration entry. `__split_folio_to_order()` in huge memory is splitting folios. `smp_wmb()` in `__split_folio_to_order()` provides an ordering guarantee that page flags of tail pages are visible before the tail page becomes non-compound. This `smp_wmb()` has to be paired with `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()`, which was initially missed. The consequence is that `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`, when it accesses a migration entry for a tail page, might see an outdated version of the `page->flags`.

Specifically, `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()` are the functions that convert a migration entry to a folio or page, respectively. They both have a check function called `softleaf_migration_entry_check()`, which now includes `smp_rmb()`. The order of operations matters: `pfn_folio()` (which reads `compound_head`) must happen *before* `smp_rmb()`, which in turn must happen *before* reading `page->flags`, like `folio_test_locked()`.

In `softleaf_to_folio()`, the `smp_rmb()` is properly placed. We read `compound_head`, execute `smp_rmb()` and the caller of `softleaf_to_folio()` will be guaranteed to see the new flags and the new state. This guarantees that `folio_test_locked()` gets the up-to-date state.

In `softleaf_to_page()`, if the check is compiled out (i.e. `CONFIG_DEBUG_VM` is off), there is an issue with ordering. The compiler will optimize out `page_folio(page)` if the returned variable is unused. Then a caller of `softleaf_to_page()` *could* later use `page_folio()` on the returned `page` and read flags. If that happens there is a possibility that a read of `compound_head` is not ordered with a read of flags.

The essential issue is that `smp_rmb()` acts as a memory barrier, ensuring that all memory accesses *before* the barrier are completed before any memory accesses *after* the barrier are initiated. So, reading `compound_head` has to happen before `smp_rmb()`, and reading `flags` has to happen after the barrier. This ensures a consistent view of the page state, especially after it's split. The warning could spuriously trigger because `folio_test_locked()` and `folio_test_dirty()` might read the old state of the page.

So, the addition of `smp_rmb()` to `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()` is necessary to fix a race condition where a process, such as `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`, might see inconsistent data (old flags and the new compound state or vice-versa) when accessing pages that are in the process of being split. The fix relies on the fact that volatile reads are not optimized away.

Okay, so I'm looking at how `zap_nonpresent_ptes` handles device-private and device-exclusive mappings. For these device-specific entries, it *does* execute `softleaf_to_page` and *then* `page_folio()`!

```c
	if (softleaf_is_device_private(entry) ||

		struct page *page = softleaf_to_page(entry);
		struct folio *folio = page_folio(page);
```

But, hold on... and this is important. Device-private and exclusive entries are *not* migration entries.  `softleaf_is_migration(entry)` will evaluate to FALSE.  This is key because it means `softleaf_to_page` *won't* call `softleaf_migration_entry_check`. Therefore, there's no `smp_rmb()` execution there.

And this is correct! Device private and exclusive entries don't have the same race conditions with THP splits as migration entries do. They don't utilize migration entries, and therefore don't need that memory barrier.

Alright, that makes sense.  Now, what happens with actual migration entries? I should keep reading `zap_nonpresent_ptes` to fully understand the flow.

Okay, so I've been digging into this potential race condition related to folio splitting in the kernel, specifically focusing on how `softleaf_to_page` is used in conjunction with `smaps_account`. The core of the problem stems from the interaction between CPU0 splitting a folio (clearing `compound_head`, setting flags, and other updates) and CPU1 reading that information via `softleaf_to_page` and then calling `smaps_account`.

The initial concern was that `softleaf_to_page`, followed by `smaps_account`'s use of `page_folio(page)`, could lead to CPU1 reading inconsistent data. Specifically, I was worried that CPU1 might observe the *new* value of `compound_head` (indicating a split) but still read the *old* values of `folio->mapping` or `folio->flags` (from before the split). This could happen because CPU might speculatively read these variables before reading `compound_head`.

I determined that this could occur due to how the `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_page` works, and is NOT a proper barrier to this reordering because the caller calls `page_folio(page)` a second time, after `smp_rmb()`.

I traced the execution flow, step-by-step: CPU0 does the split (writes data). CPU1 reads migration entries, calls `softleaf_to_page`, it executes `smp_rmb()`, which prevents reads *after* the `smp_rmb()` from being reordered *before* it. Then CPU1 calls `smaps_account`, and I am concerned about the second call to `page_folio(page)` which can result in `compound_head` being read. If the CPU reorders a read of something from the `folio` object before the second read of `compound_head`, then CPU1 could see an inconsistent view, reading the *new* `compound_head` but the *old* folio data.

I confirmed that with a good amount of tracing and re-tracing. Essentially, since CPU1's subsequent reads of `compound_head` in `smaps_account` can still race with writes from CPU0, a window exists where the CPU might optimistically prefetch and read folio's `mapping` or `flags` before the second read of `compound_head`. The initial `smp_rmb()` doesn't offer a strong enough guarantee because the caller of `softleaf_to_page` re-evaluates `compound_head` inside `page_folio(page)`.

This is a problem because if the CPU speculatively reorders these accesses, the state of the tail page may be misidentified by `smaps_account`. In `smaps_account`, this could lead to incorrect accounting of memory usage (e.g., misclassifying an anonymous page as file-backed) and potentially other memory corruption.

I drilled into various code paths using `softleaf_to_page`, like in `zap_nonpresent_ptes`, `smaps_pte_entry`. The most concerning place is `smaps_pte_entry`. It uses `softleaf_to_page` for migration entries, and calls `smaps_account` with the returned page.

The race could happen because `smaps_account` calls `page_folio(page)`, and it can incorrectly use *old* flags to make *new* data! This could result in incorrect accounting or even crashes.

I went on to double-check that this is a race and not just a speculative read, which it is. Speculative reads are allowed. Reordering reads is a specific problem.

I then realized that the current fix of the previous commit attempts to place `smp_rmb()` within `softleaf_to_page` and `softleaf_to_folio`, and while it handles some VM_WARN_ON_ONCE warnings, it does *not* address the race.

I went on to check if `softleaf_to_folio()` suffers from the race. Because it only evaluates `compound_head` once, the barrier in this function works, and the caller of `softleaf_to_folio()` does *not* suffer from this issue.

In conclusion, the `softleaf_to_page` function is problematic, and must be rewritten so as to prevent potential race conditions.

    "thought": "**Analysis of `softleaf_to_page` and Potential Race Conditions**

Okay, here's the breakdown of my thoughts on this code and the potential memory ordering issues. It seems we've got a classic read-to-read ordering problem, and the proposed fix in `softleaf_to_page` doesn't quite cut it, especially when callers immediately convert the returned `struct page*` to a `struct folio*`.

First, let's establish the context. We have several functions, like `smaps_pmd_entry`, `pagemap_pte_range`, and `copy_nonpresent_pte`, all calling `softleaf_to_page()` to retrieve a `struct page*` from a softleaf entry. The crucial part is that these callers *then* immediately call `page_folio()` on that `struct page*`. This is where things get tricky, particularly with migration entries.

Inside `softleaf_to_page`, there's an `smp_rmb()` that's meant to ensure that reads of the page's `compound_head` are properly ordered. This is good. However, and this is the crux of the problem, the caller then does a *second* read of `compound_head` within `page_folio()`. This second read isn't guaranteed to be ordered against subsequent reads of `folio->flags` or `folio->mapping` if that second read is done in the *caller*.

Let's illustrate the potential sequence of events:

1.  `softleaf_to_page()` runs. It reads `compound_head`, followed by `smp_rmb()`.
2.  The caller receives the `struct page*`.
3.  The caller calls `page_folio()`.  This reads `compound_head` AGAIN.
4.  The caller then accesses `folio->flags` or `folio->mapping`.

Now, here's where the out-of-order execution engine comes into play, specifically, speculative execution:

*   The CPU *could* predict the outcome of the branch within `page_folio()` (whether `head & 1` is true or false).
*   If the prediction leads to a direct access to `folio->flags` or `folio->mapping` (i.e. `head & 1` is false, and therefore no compound head), it's *possible* for the CPU to *speculatively* perform this read *before* the second `compound_head` read.
*   If the prediction is correct *about the branch*, the speculative execution is committed.  But, importantly, the reads are NOT ordered against each other!

This is the core of the problem. Control dependencies (the branch) do *not* guarantee read-to-read ordering.  We need a memory barrier between the *second* `compound_head` read and the subsequent folio field reads to ensure that all reads are seen in the correct order. The `smp_rmb()` inside `softleaf_to_page` only orders the *first* `compound_head` read.

The `softleaf_to_folio()` function, however, is CORRECT. It avoids the second read of `compound_head` and makes the smp_rmb effectively block the read reordering.

So the concern is: the original commit *partially* addressed the problem by including the `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_page()`, but the callers that continue to call `page_folio()` after a call to `softleaf_to_page()` are still susceptible to the race.

The fix would be to modify the callers, like `pagemap_pmd_range` and `smaps_pte_entry`, to directly use `softleaf_to_folio()` when a `struct folio*` is needed. This would eliminate the double read of `compound_head` and avoid the race.

And yes, I re-verified the address dependency analysis, and the control dependency aspect. Speculative execution in modern CPUs *can* and *does* reorder loads across branches if the data dependency can be eliminated via branch prediction. This is why we need to ensure the appropriate read-to-read ordering!

We absolutely need to ensure these callers are using `softleaf_to_folio()`!

    "thought": "**Analysis of THP Split Race Condition and Ineffective Memory Barrier**

Okay, so I've been digging into this patch, and I've found a classic race condition issue related to THP (Transparent Huge Pages) splitting. The core problem lies in how the `softleaf_to_page` function handles the conversion from a soft leaf entry to a `struct page`, especially when callers subsequently use `page_folio`. I've identified four distinct call sites with this problematic pattern, and I've also checked other relevant areas, such as device-exclusive or private entries, to make sure there are no other issues.

Here's the breakdown: When a THP splits, the tail pages have their `compound_head` cleared *after* their flags and mapping are set. Callers like `smaps_pte_entry`, `pagemap_pte_range`, and `pagemap_pmd_range` call `softleaf_to_page` to get the `page`, and then immediately call `page_folio` on the returned page, and ultimately, read fields like `folio->mapping`.

The root of the issue is that `softleaf_to_page` includes `smp_rmb()` *after* reading the `compound_head`. This barrier is supposed to enforce ordering, but it's not effective in these cases. The crucial observation is that the CPU's out-of-order execution engine can reorder *loads* that don't have a data dependency. Specifically, the CPU can speculate that the `page` isn't part of a compound page and speculatively load the `folio->mapping` before loading the `compound_head` a second time.

If the CPU does this, it might load the *old* value of `mapping` (which is `TAIL_MAPPING`), and the *new* value of `compound_head` (which is zero). When the program checks `head & 1`, it sees zero and concludes that it's a tail page. This leads to incorrect accounting and misclassification of an anonymous page as a file page.

The `smp_rmb()` inside `softleaf_to_page` is supposed to prevent this but it's *ineffective* because the *second* read of `compound_head` (inside the caller via `page_folio`) happens *after* the barrier. That second read is the crucial load that the CPU can reorder relative to `folio->mapping` reads.

The proper fix is for callers to use `softleaf_to_folio()` directly. This function reads `compound_head`, executes `smp_rmb()` and returns a `struct folio *`. All reads of `folio` fields (like `flags` or `mapping`) done after the return of `softleaf_to_folio()` are properly ordered.

The patch's attempt to fix the race by adding `smp_rmb()` inside `softleaf_to_page()` is incomplete. It silences the internal `VM_WARN_ON_ONCE` but it *doesn't* protect the vulnerable code pattern in callers that also call `page_folio`. The problem is a *read-to-read without dependency* reordering problem, caused by the return of a `struct page *` and the subsequent deferred `page_folio()` in the callers. I have written a precise JSON document to capture my concern.

I am confident in this.

Okay, so I've been wrestling with this THP migration code, and it's got a nasty little race condition lurking in there. It all boils down to the way we handle the `compound_head` field, and how the CPU might speculate reads on ARM architectures.

Here's the gist: the code uses `softleaf_to_page()` to get a `page` pointer, and then *immediately* calls `page_folio(page)` to get a `folio`. The problem is that `page_folio()` reads `page->compound_head`, which can point to either the head page of a THP or the tail page after a split.

The core of the issue is this: if a THP is in the process of being split, the CPU on one core (`CPU 0` in my thinking) might be *writing* to `compound_head` (setting it to zero when the split is finished), while another core (`CPU 1`) is *reading* `compound_head` and then reading other fields of either the head page or tail page.

The `smp_rmb()` inside `softleaf_to_page` is supposed to help, but it doesn't work the way I thought it did. Specifically, the ARM memory model permits the CPU to reorder loads.

Let me break down the critical events:

1.  **Read A (inside `softleaf_to_page`)**: CPU 1 reads `compound_head`, *before* the `smp_rmb()`.
2.  **`smp_rmb()`**: A memory barrier occurs.
3.  **Read B (inside the caller's `page_folio(page)` call)**: CPU 1 reads `compound_head` AGAIN.
4.  **Read C**: CPU 1 reads a flag (`flags`) from the *folio* (either the head or tail page)

The problem is the compiler and the CPU are allowed to speculate. On ARM, the CPU can *speculate* Read C (the flags read) *before* Read B, based on its branch prediction.

*   If the CPU predicts `compound_head == 0`, it assumes a completed split, so `folio == page`.
*   Then the CPU speculatively reads `page->flags`, which could be old/stale data.

If CPU 1 speculates, the data can be out of order. If `Read C` happens first, and gets the *old* value of `flags` (before the split), while `Read B` happens later, and sees the *new* `compound_head` (0, meaning split completed), then there's a problem.

The original patch tried to fix this with `smp_rmb()`, but that's INSUFFICIENT because there's no memory barrier between `Read B` and `Read C`! The original patch only fixed a warning, but *didn't* protect against the fundamental race.

For instance, this can lead to incorrect statistics in `/proc/pid/smaps`, where a THP might not be accounted for after a split because the speculatively read flag is incorrect.

The atomic operation (e.g., `folio_trylock`) is safe because it uses locked instructions which are not speculatively executed with stale data. However, other non-atomic reads *are* vulnerable!

The solution is to change the callers to use `softleaf_to_folio(entry)` *directly*. This function handles the *synchronization*, ensuring that `compound_head` is read *before* the `smp_rmb()`.

I've verified that the current code in `mm/huge_memory.c` and `fs/proc/task_mmu.c` is doing this unsafe pattern, repeatedly. The author just added the `smp_rmb()` and the fix is still incomplete!

The core problem is this: the compiler can move Read C before Read B, or Read C can be speculatively executed before Read B, despite the `smp_rmb()` that's supposed to protect it, and that's the vulnerability that needs addressing.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential Memory Reordering in Folio Splitting**

Okay, so I'm digging into this folio splitting logic, and it seems like there might be a subtle race condition lurking here. First, let's establish the basics. We've got `compound_info` aliasing with `lru`, which is interesting. I see `FOLIO_MATCH(compound_info, lru)`. Then, I did a quick check: `offsetof(struct page, lru)` is the same as `offsetof(struct folio, lru)`. And yes, `compound_info` *is* actually `lru`, it's an `unsigned long`.

Now, the crucial part: in `struct page`, `lru` is the *first* element of the *first* anonymous struct in the union, sitting at offset 8, right after `flags`. The second element is `mapping`, which is at offset 24, after the `lru` which takes up 16 bytes. Wait, hold on a second... `compound_info` is an `unsigned long` at offset 8! This means `compound_info` and `mapping` DO NOT OVERLAP! They're at completely different memory locations.

So, if code does:
```c
		folio = page_folio(page); // reads page->compound_info (offset 8)
		if (PageAnon(page))       // reads page->mapping (offset 24)
```

Can the CPU reorder the reads of `page->mapping` and `page->compound_info`? Yes! They are at different memory locations.

If that reordering happens, what's the consequence? The split happens, and the writer updates `page->flags` and `page->mapping`. In the tail pages, `page->mapping` should be set to `TAIL_MAPPING`. But wait, in the `softleaf_to_page(entry)` function, there's an `smp_rmb()` that's already executed *before* the caller reads both values.

So, the caller *should* be reading `page->mapping` and `page->compound_info` AFTER that `smp_rmb()`. If it's reading *after* the `smp_rmb()`, then it's guaranteed to see the state that was visible when `smp_rmb()` executed, or later. Right?

Let's look at the writer's perspective:
```c
__split_folio_to_order()
{
    // ...
    set flags for tail pages
    smp_wmb()
    for tail pages:
        clear compound_info (make it 0, meaning not a tail page)
        set mapping (if needed)
}
```

Okay, here's where it gets interesting. The writer updates `mapping` *after* the `smp_wmb()`. The caller *might* read the OLD `mapping` (which will be `TAIL_MAPPING`) but the NEW `compound_info` (which will say it's not a tail page anymore).

If it sees the new `compound_info`, it will treat it as a head page. But it might read the old `mapping` (TAIL_MAPPING). So, `PageAnon(page)` will read `TAIL_MAPPING`.

Wait a minute... if it reads `TAIL_MAPPING` instead of the *actual* mapping, that's a bug! This is a classic race. This is why memory barriers exist!

So, the question is: is `mapping` updated AFTER `smp_wmb()`? Looks like it! Time to dive into `prep_compound_page` and `__split_folio_to_order()` to confirm.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Page Splitting and the `PageAnon` Function**

Okay, so I'm looking closely at how `__split_folio_to_order` works. Specifically, I'm concerned with the memory ordering and how it interacts with the `mapping` field of a `struct page`, especially in the context of compound pages (folios). I notice the code sets `new_folio->mapping` before a `smp_wmb()` memory barrier. The immediate thought is, if a reader comes along *after* that barrier, will it see the correct, new `mapping`? Seems like it should. But... wait. What if the page *was* a tail page *before* the split? Tail pages aren't supposed to have a valid `mapping` field, right? They use `compound_head` instead, which is a different field altogether.

My memory of older kernels is surfacing. I recall `TAIL_MAPPING` as a concept, but in modern ones, `mapping` is just a pointer to an `address_space`. So, does a tail page even *have* a `mapping` field? Yes, it does. It's just usually NULL or garbage because it's not used. Ah, right. The code does `new_folio->mapping = folio->mapping;` *before* the `smp_wmb()`. That means the reader should see the correct value, either the new one after the split, or the old one if the split hasn't occurred yet.

That's when it hits me: what if the reader gets a pointer to the *tail* page before the split? Its `mapping` field is undefined, isn't it? Garbage.

Let's switch gears and investigate how `PageAnon(page)` handles this. It casts `page` to `folio` and checks `folio->mapping`. If `page` is a tail page, `folio->mapping` is just `page->mapping`. Which, as we established, could be anything. So, `PageAnon` could return garbage if given a tail page! But wait! `PageAnon` *shouldn't* be called on tail pages. It's supposed to be called on head pages! "Any use of migration entries may only occur while the corresponding page is locked." Meaning `PageAnon` isn't designed to deal with a tail page! The code should call `folio_test_anon(folio)` instead! That seems like a potential bug in any caller that does `PageAnon(page)` with `page` being a tail page!

Now I think I see a code example: `mm/huge_memory.c` where there's a problematic call to `PageAnon(page)`. I have to remember that migration entries *do* store PFNs for tail pages when a large folio is mapped with PTEs! Each PTE contains a migration entry referencing a subpage (which is a tail page) when you have a folio mapped with PTEs. That means `entry` stores a tail PFN, and `page` is a tail page. And *then* `PageAnon(page)` is called! Is this safe? I'm not so sure...

    "thought": "**The Race Condition in PageAnon During Folio Splitting**

Okay, so it looks like `PageAnon` calls `page_folio`, which in turn reads `compound_head`. But if `page_folio` does the `compound_head` read AGAIN, after an `smp_rmb`, will there be a race? No, because there's a data dependency between the read of `compound_head` and the subsequent read of `folio->mapping`! The compiler *has* to respect this.  The CPU *won't* reorder the reads in a way that breaks things.

However, the core issue seems to be with speculative execution and control dependencies. The key is how `_compound_head` works. If the lower bit of `compound_head` is *not* set, it returns the *original* page pointer, which is the input of function!  The CPU *can* predict this and read `folio->mapping` *before* it reads `compound_head` when running `_compound_head()`, because reads aren't ordered by a control dependency.

Now let's apply that to `PageAnon(page)`. It calls `page_folio(page)`, which ends up in `_compound_head`. `PageAnon(page)` then reads `folio->mapping`. This means the CPU COULD reorder the reads, and therefore, it's possible to read `folio->mapping` before `compound_head`! But does this happen? The read of `compound_head` may be speculative.

If the caller already did an `smp_rmb`, *before* the call to `PageAnon`, that's good. But the reads within `PageAnon` themselves don't have an `smp_rmb`. This means that `PageAnon` *could* read an old value for `mapping`, and then a *new* value for `compound_head`, potentially leading to a bug, especially when dealing with split pages.  Specifically, the reads within `PageAnon` are *not* ordered by the compiler!

The writer writes the mapping, does an `smp_wmb()`, and then clears the `compound_head`. The reader reads the `mapping` and then reads the `compound_head`. But without ordering, the speculative read of `mapping` can occur before the value is written!  This message-passing pattern is vulnerable without `smp_rmb` between the reads. This is an example of why speculative execution can break memory ordering guarantees.

So the original bug wasn't in `PageAnon` itself, but in the lack of memory ordering when calling it. The key is understanding that during a folio split, `page->mapping` and `page->compound_head` are modified concurrently. The code was using `folio_test_locked`, without a barrier.

The key to fix it is using memory barriers to ensure the read of `mapping` happens *after* the read of `compound_head`, not just in the call to `folio_test_locked()`, but in *every* call!

Ah! It looks like `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` calls `softleaf_to_folio(entry)` instead of `PageAnon()`.

Since `softleaf_to_folio(entry)` *does not* include an `smp_rmb`, there's no guarantee the compiler will respect the data dependency!  The code *can* read garbage for `mapping`.

    "thought": "**Analyzing Potential Race Conditions in Page Migration Code**

Okay, so I've been digging into this page migration code, particularly around the interactions with `softleaf_to_folio()` and `PageAnon()`. Initially, I was concerned about a potential race condition in `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`. The concern was this: if `softleaf_to_folio()` reads a `compound_head` value and then `mm_counter(folio)` is called *after* an `smp_rmb()`, is the code safe? After digging in, I determined yes, the fix for `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` is actually correct. This is because `mm_counter(folio)` will read `folio->mapping` on the *head* page, after a `smp_rmb()`.

However, my attention then shifted to other callers of `softleaf_to_page()`, especially within `mm/huge_memory.c`. Specifically, the code calling `PageAnon(page)` after calling `softleaf_to_page(entry)`. The key here is that `PageAnon(page)` indirectly reads the `mapping` field of a `page` via `page_folio()`. Without careful synchronization, it seemed possible for these reads to be reordered by the CPU, resulting in reading an older `mapping` value before a newly written `compound_head`, potentially leading to `PageAnon()` returning an incorrect result. That seems *inherently* racy to me!

After deeper analysis, I realized that for HEAD pages, there *is* a data dependency between the read of `compound_head` and the read of `mapping` via `folio_test_anon`. However, if the page is a tail page, the `mapping` field will be initialized to a garbage value, and there is no data dependency! Since the `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_page()` only prevents reordering of reads after the barrier with reads *before* the barrier, but allows reordering of reads after the barrier, this opens up a race condition!

The critical moment happens during THP splits. The writer updates the `mapping` and `compound_head` fields, and `smp_wmb()` orders them. The reader is inside `softleaf_to_page()` after an `smp_rmb()`, which can allow a speculative read to get a bad value of `mapping`. This could lead `PageAnon()` to return an incorrect result for tail pages, and `anon_exclusive` could be incorrectly set. This would be a nasty bug!

However, the real solution here is: this *only* applies to PTE entries, not PMD entries. The `page` pointer in `mm/huge_memory.c` refers to the HEAD page of the THP! Therefore, the read of the `mapping` field in `PageAnon()` is safe. When the THP is unmapped, `make_migration_entry` is called with the HEAD page's PFN. Also, when THPs split, it only modifies the tail pages, but not the head page. The `mapping` of the head page *remains* valid. The `compound_head` field of the head page also remains unchanged. Therefore the `PageAnon` call is safe.

So in summary, the only scenario where this would be unsafe is when `zap_nonpresent_ptes` handles the PTE, since the PFN *could* point to a tail page.

After going over `mm/memory.c`, there are no other callers of `softleaf_to_page()` that also call `PageAnon(page)` for a PTE entry.

So the code is safe!

    "thought": "**Deep Dive into `device_exclusive` and Memory Management**

Okay, let's break this down. Initially, I was focusing on `try_restore_exclusive_pte` in `mm/memory.c`, and I was getting tripped up by the assumption that it was related to page migration. But wait a minute! This is *device_exclusive*, not migration! These are two different beasts.

The key realization is that `softleaf_is_migration()` is *false* for `device_exclusive` entries. This means `softleaf_to_page()` in this context *doesn't* execute `smp_rmb()` and that means we are not subject to the hazards of a migration. So the concerns I was having about the race condition that `__split_folio_to_order` causes here don't apply.

Then, the question of whether a large folio with `device_exclusive` entries can be split comes up. The answer is *no*. `device_exclusive` entries are almost exclusively, if not entirely, for `ZONE_DEVICE` or potentially anonymous pages. The refcount is the deciding factor, since `device_exclusive` takes a reference on the page, and the refcount can not be exactly as needed to split the page. So, this further narrows down the scope of the problem.

Actually, the `softleaf_to_page` will only call `smp_rmb` if `softleaf_is_migration`.

Then, I shifted my attention to another section, `mm/memory.c:4802`. Here, it's clear: when we have `softleaf_is_migration`, we *don't* call `softleaf_to_page()`. Instead, we call `migration_entry_wait()`. This is another key distinction.

I should also investigate `fs/proc/task_mmu.c`, as this might provide further context.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of `softleaf_to_page` and Potential Data Races**

Okay, let's break this down. My primary concern here is data dependencies and how the CPU might reorder memory accesses, especially around page migration and the use of `softleaf_to_page`.

Initially, I looked at how `smaps_account` and similar functions use `struct page *` and found no immediate issues because dependencies on the `folio` (and thus `compound_head`) were correctly established through function calls. There's no speculative read of the `mapping` field before checking `compound_head` when the functions that could cause the race use `folio`. `PageAnon(page)` is the potential pitfall, but as long as we use a function like `folio_test_anon(folio)`, it should be safe.

I identified the key area for potential issues as the use of `PageAnon(page)` or similar macros which directly access page flags after calling `softleaf_to_page`.  This is used in a few places, specifically, when checking PMD migration entries and device-private entries.

However, I realized that for PMD migration entries, the PFN is always the head page.  Since the migration entry is always a head page, `page->compound_head` never changes from tail to head, and the reads are always valid. Even if `PageAnon(page)` reorders the reads, both reads are valid. The write to `mapping` is always valid for a head page. Therefore, the race does not exist here.

The real danger lies with PTE migration entries.  If we're dealing with a PTE migration entry, then the PFN *can* be the tail page of a THP. The caller could read OLD `compound_head`, THEN read NEW `flags`. The new patch replaces the old check for `PageLocked` with a new function `softleaf_migration_entry_check(entry, page_folio(page))`.

The call to `page_folio(page)` within `softleaf_to_page()` reads `page->compound_head` before the `smp_rmb()`. The subsequent call to `folio_test_locked(folio)` reads `folio->flags`. The `smp_rmb()` enforces a memory barrier, ensuring that the read of `folio->flags` does *not* get reordered before the read of `compound_head`. This prevents a speculative read of `folio->flags` before we are sure of the value of `compound_head`.

If the reader reads `compound_head` FIRST, it gets the NEW value and NEW flags. If it sees the NEW value, the writer's writes *must* be visible because of the `smp_wmb()`.

I had to ensure that the read of `compound_head` happens BEFORE the read of `flags`! The barrier `smp_rmb()` is essential. Since `compound_head` is read BEFORE the barrier, and `flags` is read AFTER, then it is safe. `smp_rmb()` enforces the correct ordering, preventing the race condition.

I then considered what happens if the reader gets the OLD `compound_head`.  In that case, the folio lock is set, so no warning is triggered. If they get the NEW `compound_head`, then the read of `flags` will show the NEW state.

In general, `smp_rmb()` in `softleaf_to_page` protects the callers, but only if they correctly use the returned value to access the members of the folio!

I considered that a caller may call `softleaf_to_page(entry)`, then `page_folio(page)`, then `PageAnon(page)`, and this is where it can race. Even if the barrier in the `softleaf_to_page` prevents the race in there, can it be raced between the return from `softleaf_to_page` and use of the returned value? There's no barrier, nor data dependency, for callers of `softleaf_to_page`.

So if the reader speculatively executes the call to the function, it will get the old state, but if the reader does not speculatively execute, it is fine. It also means that a caller could read `compound_head` and `mapping` in the wrong order. This can cause the read of the old `mapping` with the new value of `compound_head` . But this is only a concern for PTE migration entries.

Then I carefully examined callers of `softleaf_to_page()` to identify potential bugs. After reviewing the code, I concluded that there are *no* callers of `softleaf_to_page()` that handle PTE migration entries and are susceptible to this race. The existing callers all handle either device private or exclusive entries

    "thought": "**Analysis of `zap_nonpresent_ptes` and THP Splitting with Migration Entries**

Okay, here's my understanding of this code and the potential race condition. In `zap_nonpresent_ptes`, when dealing with migration entries related to a page that's undergoing a THP split, we're essentially walking a tightrope of memory visibility. My concern is the interaction between the split process (`__split_folio_to_order`) and `zap_nonpresent_ptes` trying to clean up PTEs for pages that are now part of a split THP.

The core of the issue boils down to the `compound_head` field of the folio and the `PG_locked` flag. `zap_nonpresent_ptes` sees a migration entry, calls `softleaf_to_folio`, which ultimately reads the `compound_head`. The split happens *after* the migration entry is created, so the migration entry's PFN is for what *was* a tail page of a THP but is *now* a tail page of a smaller THP.

Here's where the memory barrier (`smp_rmb()`) becomes crucial:

*   **Without `smp_rmb()`:** The CPU executing `zap_nonpresent_ptes` might read the *new* `compound_head` (pointing to the split folio, which is the tail page's new head) but *old* flags for that new head, potentially *before* the `PG_locked` flag is set by the splitting thread. This would lead to a false negative for the `folio_test_locked` test, causing the `VM_WARN_ON_ONCE` to trigger.
*   **With `smp_rmb()`:** The barrier ensures that any write to `compound_head` is visible before any subsequent read to any field of the *same* folio. If the migration entry still points to the old head page, the folio is locked, so we will not get a warning. If the migration entry now points to the new head page, we see the new `compound_head` value and then the updated `flags` that include `PG_locked`.     "thought": "**Analysis of Migration Entry Race Conditions**

Okay, let's break this down. My initial thought was about how `check_pte` in `page_vma_mapped_walk` doesn't actually call `softleaf_to_folio()` or `softleaf_to_page()`. Instead, it just checks the PFN against what's expected. Then, I realized that if a migration entry *is* encountered, `page_vma_mapped_walk` needs the `PVMW_MIGRATION` flag set to return true, which means the caller is expected to handle it.

Then, I was thinking about how `page_mapped_in_vma()` uses `page_folio()` but is called in the context of hardware-poisoned pages.  Crucially, the folio *must* be locked in this context which prevents any concurrent split, so the use of `page_folio()` here is safe.

That led me to the `VM_WARN_ON_ONCE` check in `softleaf_migration_entry_check()`.  The comment clearly states that migration entries should *only* be accessed while the corresponding folio is locked. I wanted to verify how the locking and unlocking are handled during migration.

So, how are migration entries created and removed?  The migration process is responsible. And, yes, it *does* lock the folio before inserting the migration entries. And it *does* unlock it *after*! Specifically, both `migrate_folio_move()` and `unmap_and_move()` lock the old folio, insert entries, *then* unlock the folio. I thought that maybe in `unmap_and_move_huge_page()` there might be a problem, but it locks the *old* folio, inserts entries, copies data, sets up new PTEs, then unlocks *both* folios.

My next concern: what if `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` encounters a migration entry *after* the folio has been unlocked but *before* the entry is removed? That’s where `remove_migration_ptes()` comes in.  `remove_migration_ptes()` is called while the folio *is* locked, meaning the folio is always locked while the migration entries exist! In `unmap_and_move()`, `remove_migration_ptes()` is called, and *then* the folio is unlocked. Same with `__split_folio_to_order()`.

However, the more detailed review of `__split_folio_to_order()` raised an interesting point. In that function `remove_migration_ptes()` is called but on the `new_folio`, which are unlocked after the function returns, not before! So inside `__split_folio_to_order()` ALL new folios are still LOCKED! Meaning, `folio_test_locked()` is true as expected. `split_folio()` unlocks the new folios *after* the loop in `__split_folio_to_order()` is done.

So, it's pretty clear: there's no path where the migration entries could exist while the folio is unlocked. The locking scheme is strictly enforced.

Finally, I checked `softleaf_to_page()`. `page_folio(page)` evaluates to `_compound_head(page)`. For the migration entry pointing to a tail page that's being split, `page_folio()` reads `compound_head`. `softleaf_migration_entry_check()` is not invoked in this case. Also, in the case of exclusive/private device pages, `softleaf_is_migration(entry)` is false, so no `smp_rmb()` and no race here.

Therefore, this patch should be correct regarding the race conditions involved, and I don't see any obvious new issues it introduces.

However, `__split_folio_to_order` modifies the `compound_head` and `flags` of tail pages, and crucially, *without* holding the PTE lock. It does hold the folio lock, but it's not enough to prevent all races. Because the migration entries are inserted by `unmap_folio()` and the PTL is released. So `__split_folio_to_order` *is* indeed running concurrently!

Now, the `smp_rmb()` in `copy_nonpresent_pte` is crucial. It guarantees that if `copy_nonpresent_pte` reads the *new* `compound_head`, it *also* sees the *new* `flags`, `mapping`, etc. This seems to be correct. And the `rss[mm_counter(folio)]++` will then use the *correct* `mapping`, either the old one or the new one. Either way, the anon/file status is the same.

Wait a second… is there still a problem? `mm_counter(folio)` relies on `folio_test_anon(folio)`. If that `smp_rmb()` *wasn't* there, `copy_nonpresent_pte` could see the *new* `compound_head` but a *stale* `mapping` (maybe NULL). In that case, `folio_test_anon(folio)` would return false, leading to the RSS counter being incremented incorrectly for `MM_FILEPAGES` instead of `MM_ANONPAGES`! Right, if it reads a stale `mapping` which is NULL, `folio_test_anon` returns false! That would *absolutely* corrupt the RSS counters!

So, that `smp_rmb()` is not just good – it's *essential*. It fixes a potential RSS corruption during `fork()`! That's a subtle but critical race condition. I'll need to check the code around `mm/migrate_device.c:305` too, to make sure this is consistent everywhere. This is a good catch.

Alright, let's break down this patch. My initial thought? The `smp_wmb()` and `smp_rmb()` pairing seems perfectly sound in `__split_folio_to_order()` and the added `softleaf_migration_entry_check()`. No immediate red flags. I'm focusing on those critical memory barrier rules, you know: preventing deadlocks, ensuring all unlocks are handled correctly, checking for any sleeping in atomic contexts, making sure we aren't misusing RCU, and ensuring memory barriers are properly implemented when we're dealing with lockless concurrency.

Okay, so I don't see any deadlocks or overlooked unlocks in the error paths. No new sleeping functions have been added, and RCU isn't in play here, so the "CRITICAL RCU RULE" is a non-issue. The code *adds* an `smp_rmb()`, which is a positive sign – we're explicitly addressing lockless concurrency. It perfectly complements the existing `smp_wmb()` in `__split_folio_to_order()`, targeting the race between reading a pointer and its fields. The barrier is placed where it *should* be: after reading `compound_head` (which provides the `folio` pointer) and before reading `folio->flags`. This suggests the patch is trying to fix a memory ordering issue.

Now, let's drill down: Is `smp_rmb()` the right barrier? Yes, writers use `smp_wmb()`, readers use `smp_rmb()`. Is it in the right place? Absolutely, it's guarding access to `folio->flags` after the pointer has been updated. The function argument passing to `softleaf_migration_entry_check()` is safe because function arguments are fully evaluated before the function call, enforcing the order. Thus, `page_folio(page)` happens before, then `smp_rmb()`, and then `folio_test_locked(folio)`. But, I've got to make sure the compiler doesn't sneak in an optimization!

The compiler can't reorder things here because `folio_test_locked()` is an inline function, so it's a compiler barrier. That stops the compiler from moving the read of `folio->flags` *before* the `smp_rmb()`. And the whole argument to `softleaf_migration_entry_check()` is itself a compiler barrier! The caller to `softleaf_to_page` is:

```c
static inline struct page *softleaf_to_page(softleaf_t entry)
{
	struct page *page = pfn_to_page(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));

	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
		softleaf_migration_entry_check(entry, page_folio(page));

	return page;
}
```

Is `page_folio(page)` evaluated before the `smp_rmb()`? Yes, C semantics guarantees that! Even in an inline function, argument evaluation is ordered. The read of `page->compound_head` uses `READ_ONCE`, which makes it `volatile`, so it can't be reordered. The `READ_ONCE` and `smp_rmb()` are opaque to the compiler for reordering.

So the argument is evaluated before the barrier, and `folio->flags` is read after the barrier. This means the compiler ordering is absolutely guaranteed.

Let's check `softleaf_to_folio(entry)` to be complete:

```c
static inline struct folio *softleaf_to_folio(softleaf_t entry)
{
	struct folio *folio = pfn_folio(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));

	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
		softleaf_migration_entry_check(entry, folio);

	return folio;
}
```

Here, the local `folio` is assigned and then passed to the barrier-guarded function, again a guaranteed correct ordering.

The patch *refactors* the warning into the function `softleaf_migration_entry_check` and adds an `smp_rmb()` inside. This is a fix for the race.

Author: Jinjiang Tu <tujinjiang@huawei.com>

mm/huge_memory: fix folio isn't locked in softleaf_to_folio()

This commit adds an smp_rmb() memory barrier in softleaf_to_folio() and
softleaf_to_page() to fix a race condition between mTHP splitting and
zap_nonpresent_ptes() where page flags of tail pages could be read before
the tail page becomes non-compound.

>  	return swp_offset(entry) & SWP_PFN_MASK;
>  }

> +			struct folio *folio)

> +	smp_rmb();
> +
> +	/*
> +	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
> +	 * corresponding page is locked
> +	 */
> +	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio));
> +}

>  	struct page *page = pfn_to_page(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
>  
>  	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
> -	/*
> -	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
> -	 * corresponding page is locked
> -	 */
> -	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(softleaf_is_migration(entry) && !PageLocked(page));
> +	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
> +		softleaf_migration_entry_check(entry, page_folio(page));
>  
>  	return page;
>  }

Does this smp_rmb() inside softleaf_to_page() (via the call to
softleaf_migration_entry_check) fully protect callers that subsequently
re-derive the folio from the returned page?

If a caller uses softleaf_to_page() and then immediately calls
page_folio(page) or PageAnon(page), that performs a new read of
page->compound_head after the smp_rmb() has already executed.

Could the CPU speculatively reorder subsequent reads (like folio->mapping
or folio->flags) before this second read of compound_head, recreating the
exact read-read reordering race this commit intended to fix?

For example, if __split_huge_pmd_locked() or smaps_pte_entry() uses
softleaf_to_page() and then checks PageAnon(page), it might read a stale
TAIL_MAPPING before the new compound_head.

Should these callers be updated to use softleaf_to_folio() instead of
softleaf_to_page() so they use the safely-synchronized folio pointer?

Okay, here's how I see this patch. The goal is to address a potential race condition in the huge memory management code, specifically related to mTHP splitting and `zap_nonpresent_ptes()`. I need to formulate a clear, LKML-style response to the proposed patch. The input details are as follows: Commit: `2ff19b031c2f62bb18bc5818d6d8b6553e523364`, Author: Jinjiang Tu, Subject: `mm/huge_memory: fix folio isn't locked in softleaf_to_folio()`, and the brief description is: "This commit adds an `smp_rmb()` memory barrier in `softleaf_to_folio()` and `softleaf_to_page()` to fix a race condition between mTHP splitting and `zap_nonpresent_ptes()` where page flags could be read before `compound_head`."

The crux of the issue is this: the patch introduces an `smp_rmb()` to ensure memory ordering when dealing with migration entries.  However, I'm concerned about the effectiveness of this barrier in `softleaf_to_page()`. Specifically, if a caller immediately re-derives the folio after calling `softleaf_to_page()` (e.g., using `page_folio(page)` or `PageAnon(page)`), there's a subsequent unprotected read of `page->compound_head`.

Here's the relevant code snippet, including the `softleaf_migration_entry_check()` function:

```c
+static inline void softleaf_migration_entry_check(softleaf_t entry,
+			struct folio *folio)
+{
+	/* See __split_folio_to_order() comment */
+	smp_rmb();
+
+	/*
+	 * Any use of migration entries may only occur while the
+	 * corresponding page is locked
+	 */
+	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!folio_test_locked(folio));
+}
+
+/**
+ * softleaf_to_page() - Obtains struct page for PFN encoded within leaf entry.
+ * @entry: Leaf entry, softleaf_has_pfn(@entry) must return true.
+ *
+ * Context: Any.
+ *
+ * Return: struct page pointer for the given leaf entry.
+ */
+static inline struct page *softleaf_to_page(softleaf_t entry)
+{
+	struct page *page = pfn_to_page(softleaf_to_pfn(entry));
+
+	VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(!softleaf_has_pfn(entry));
+	if (softleaf_is_migration(entry))
+		softleaf_migration_entry_check(entry, page_folio(page));
+
+	return page;
+}
```

Does this `smp_rmb()` inside `softleaf_to_page()` (via the call to
`softleaf_migration_entry_check`) fully protect callers that subsequently
re-derive the folio from the returned page?

If a caller uses `softleaf_to_page()` and then immediately calls
`page_folio(page)` or `PageAnon(page)`, that performs a new read of
`page->compound_head` after the `smp_rmb()` has already executed.

Could the CPU speculatively reorder subsequent reads (like `folio->mapping`
or `folio->flags`) before this second read of `compound_head`, recreating the
exact read-read reordering race this commit intended to fix?

For example, if `__split_huge_pmd_locked()` or `smaps_pte_entry()` uses
`softleaf_to_page()` and then checks `PageAnon(page)`, it might read a stale
`TAIL_MAPPING` before the new `compound_head`.

Should these callers be updated to use `softleaf_to_folio()` instead of
`softleaf_to_page()` so they use the safely-synchronized folio pointer?
